data.title: Trump nominates Christopher Phelan to head the Council of Economic Advisers
data.description:
President Donald Trump nominated Christopher Phelan to be the chairman of the Council of Economic Advisers, replacing Stephen Miran, who resigned when his stint as a Federal Reserve governor extended past February.

Phelan is a  professor of economics at the University of Minnesota and an adviser to the Federal Reserve Bank of Minneapolis's Research Department, the district bank said on its website.

The administration submitted Phelan's nomination on Tuesday, according to a statement.

Phelan received his AB and AM degrees in economics and computer science from Duke University and the University of Chicago. In 1990, he received a PhD in economics from the University of Chicago. His research agenda focuses on dynamic contracting, government reputation, game theory, and macroeconomics, the Minneapolis Fed said.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Iran says it has ‘received signs’ that that U.S. is ready to stop the blockade – report
data.description:
Iran’s UN Ambassador Amir Saeid Iravani said Tuesday that negotiations with the U.S. will resume as soon as the naval blockade ends, calling the blockade a violation of the ceasefire, AP News reported.

“I think the next round of the negotiations will take place in Islamabad,” the ambassador said, reiterating that lifting the blockade is a condition for new talks.

When asked about the possibility of military escalation, Iravani signaled Iran’s readiness for either outcome.

“If they want to go to the war, in this case also Iran is ready for that,” he told reporters at U.N. headquarters in New York, though he added, “We should give the chance—we hope.”

The warning came as a two-week truce is set to expire Wednesday, with last-minute ceasefire talks looking increasingly uncertain.

Vice President JD Vance’s trip to Pakistan for potential talks is on hold, according to a U.S. official, and Iran has not yet decided whether to join any new negotiations.

U.S. President Donald Trump told CNBC that he expects the U.S. to “end up with a great deal” with Iran to end the war. He also said he does not expect to extend the ongoing ceasefire, which will expire on Wednesday.

“I think they have no choice,” he said.

More on the U.S.-Iran war

  • Vance’s Islamabad trip on hold as Iran fails to respond to U.S. terms
  • JD Vance, U.S. delegation to land in Pakistan on Wednesday for talks with Iran- reports
  • Trump signals no extension of truce ahead of scheduled peace talks; oil down
  • Trump threatens again to blow up all Iran's bridges and power plants
  • When will Hormuz shipping normalize? See what traders are predicting

data.title: There is need for improvement in terms of centralization of Fed operations, says Fed's Waller
data.description:
There is a need to do more to centralize operations at the 12 Reserve Banks into national lines of business, according to Federal Reserve Board Governor Christopher Waller.

Waller was speaking on "Modernizing Reserve Bank Operations" before the Brookings Institution.

"I see no reason to reduce the number of Reserve Banks or alter their geographic boundaries. Each Bank president still has an independent voice at the FOMC on the appropriate course of monetary policy, and that should continue," he said.

"At the beginning, everything a Reserve Bank did was 'local'—no national functions were performed," said Waller.

"This decentralized approach made more sense when the economy and the banking system were much more regional in nature, but as finance and the economy became more national in scope, changes were needed," added the Fed governor.

"In this era, Reserve Banks operated under a clear 'Bank first, System second' mindset," he noted.

"Over the decades, as technology changed and U.S. financial sector regulation evolved, the external environment began to shift in important ways," Waller said.

"The Reserve Banks saw a significant reduction in operations and employment at their branches, to the point of closing and selling some buildings. Even at head offices, the automation of check processing and other labor-intensive payments work reduced manpower needs and employment," said Waller.

Citing examples of centralization, Waller cited Fed financial services consolidated into a single national payment service line and the Fed's role as fiscal agent for the Treasury, handling payments and securities issuance, auctions, and redemptions, as well as providing other banking services.

"Despite this gradual movement toward more centralization in payments, IT, and fiscal agency, many support functions, including HR, procurement, and finance, are still run more or less separately at each of the 12 Reserve Banks. In my view, we have reached a point where we need to better exploit the efficiency and risk reduction benefits of standardizing and probably centrally leading all of these functions," said Waller.

With these functions, the Fed's philosophy must be "System first, Bank second," according to the Fed governor.

Developing... Check back for updates.

data.title: Autodesk remains mostly insulated from AI disruption, Middle East conflict: Oppenheimer
data.description:
Autodesk's (ADSK) business remains mostly insulated from the persistent AI disruption narrative, and its diversification strategy should also help it more easily navigate the current conflict in the Middle East, according to Oppenheimer.

"Regarding AI, we were reminded that Autodesk's advantages of proprietary data, end-market context, IP/data permissions, and critical data/IP safeguards will be difficult for startups to replicate," said Oppenheimer analysts, led by Ken Wong, in an investor note.

Instead, Autodesk views AI as a tailwind, as it can help modernize the architecture, engineering, and construction technology stack.

Although the AI disruption narrative might not affect its revenue, Autodesk shares have declined 17% year to date.

"Autodesk isn't immune to macro shocks, catering to end markets sensitive to the economy," Wong noted. "However, ADSK's diversification provides some resiliency. The Middle East accounts for a mid-single-digit percentage of revenue, and the business is well diversified across geographies, end markets, and customer sizes. If macro risks escalate, we see potential for management to de-risk the full-year outlook, similar to FY26 guidance."

Oppenheimer maintains its Outperform rating and $325 price target on the stock.

"We expect Autodesk to deliver durable high-teens to low 20% earnings growth powered by HSD-LDD revenue growth and consistent margin expansion in the mid- to long term," Wong added. "We also believe Autodesk is well positioned to capitalize on the digitization of the construction market, which remains a large ($12B TAM) analog end-market."

data.title: A smoking ban in the UK for people born after 2008 becomes a reality
data.description:
The UK's House of Commons and the House of Lords agreed on the final text of the highly watched Tobacco and Vapes Bill. The legislation creates what the government calls a “smokefree generation” by making it permanently illegal to sell tobacco products to anyone born on or after January 1, 2009. That cutoff means anyone currently 17 or younger will never legally be able to purchase cigarettes, cigars, herbal smoking products, or cigarette papers in the UK. The legal age of sale will then rise by one year each year starting in 2027, so the restriction expands permanently forward in time.

Government ministers will gain new powers under the legislation to regulate the flavors, packaging, and advertising of vaping and other consumer nicotine products, with an eye toward preventing products from being marketed to appeal to children. Adults who buy vaping or nicotine products on behalf of minors will also face fines. Retail licensing requirements for nicotine product sellers are expected as well. The bill now heads to royal assent to become law, which is now a constitutional formality rather than a real veto sticking point. 

The tobacco companies that will be most impacted by the sweeping tobacco bill are Imperial Brands (IMBBY) (IMBBF) and British American Tobacco (BTI) (BTAFF), given their combined dominance of UK cigarette sales. Japan Tobacco International (JAPAY) (JAPAF) and Philip Morris International (PM) will see their long-term UK combustible volumes structurally eroded as the generational ban takes hold.

data.title: Amazon One Medical launches GLP-1 management program
data.description:
• Members of the Amazon (AMZN) One Medical primary care health service, as well as cash-pay customers, can access obesity medications such as Novo Nordisk's (NVO) Wegovy (semaglutide) and Eli Lilly's (LLY) Zepbound (tirzepatide) and Foundayo (orforglipron) through a new GLP-1 management program.
  • The offering provides users with primary care, pharmacy services, and virtual care options to support weight loss. 
  • Under the program, individuals receive a pre-visit screening, structured consultations, regular follow-up visits with monitoring, documentation, and evidence-based treatment algorithms that include built-in safety protocols. 
  • Cash-pay customers will pay as low as $299 for injectable meds and as low as $149 a month for pills through Amazon Pharmacy.

data.title: Ramaco Resources on the rise as Goldman upgrades after stock's YTD weakness
data.description:
Ramaco Resources (METC) up 8.1% in Tuesday's trading as Goldman Sachs upgraded the metallurgical coal producer to Neutral from Sell with a $15 price target, ticked up from $14, pointing to increasing met coal assumptions on improved market dynamics.

Goldman's Brian Lee said his Neutral rating is supported by Ramaco's (METC) valuation coming down to more palpable levels, with the analyst attributing the weakness to operational shifts in its rare earths business, which he had initially viewed as causing the company to be relatively overvalued.

More importantly, Ramaco's (METC) strategic shift within its rare earths segment has the company looking to de-emphasize its reliance on scandium production, which was a key part of Goldman's Sell thesis, Lee wrote.

Additionally, the analyst said dynamics within the coal industry have seen some recent improvements, which he now believes can support a more rapid expansion of its coal assets and drive improved EBITDA generation.

As a result, Lee said he views Ramaco's (METC) continued share price decline as providing less downside potential, supported by its core coal business.

data.title: Equifax stock slips after maintaining revenue guidance following impressive Q1 beat
data.description:
Equifax (EFX) stock dipped 1.7% in Tuesday afternoon trading after the firm posted strong earnings for the first quarter of the year but kept its full-year revenue growth guidance unchanged due to an uncertain outlook.

Even with the strong Q1 results, the provider of credit scores maintained its revenue growth guidance due to uncertainty in the global macro picture and lower U.S. mortgage activity that resulted from higher rates since the Iran conflict began.

Q1 adjusted EPS of $1.86, beating the average analyst estimate of $1.70, rose from $1.53 in the year-ago quarter.

Q1 revenue of $1.65B, topping the $1.62B consensus, increased 14% from $1.44B a year ago. Workforce Solutions revenue of $683.1M rose 10% Y/Y, while U.S. Information Solutions revenue of $605.6M increased 21%. Total International revenue of $360.2M rose 4%.

"The revenue outperformance was principally driven by very strong U.S. mortgage revenue growth of 38%, principally in January and February before rates increased from the Iran conflict, continued solid performance in diversified markets up 6% in local currency, and strong new product innovation momentum with Vitality Index of 17% — significantly above our long-term goal of 10%, CEO Mark W. Begor said.

Q1 operating expenses of $1.36B grew from $1.21B in last year's Q1.

Adjusted EBITDA  of $477.4M, exceeding the $457.5M consensus, increased 13% Y/Y. Adjusted EBITDA margin was 29.0%, down slightly from 29.3% in the year-ago period.

data.title: MSCI expects Q2 2026 Analytics revenue growth of roughly 5% while reiterating 18%-20% Q2 tax rate outlook
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: MSCI Inc. (MSCI) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “Our key financial metrics included organic revenue growth of over 13%, adjusted EPS growth of nearly 14%, adjusted EBITDA growth of almost 19%.” (Chairman, CEO & President Henry Fernandez)
  • “Between January 1 and yesterday, we repurchased more than $464 million of MSCI shares at an average price of about $556 per share.” (Chairman, CEO & President Fernandez)
  • “Our Q1 operating metrics included total run rate growth of nearly 13%, fueled by a record asset-based fee run rate of $872 million, growing 25%; and recurring subscription run rate growth of 9%, fueled by net new recurring subscription sales of $39.6 million, growing 52%.” (Chairman, CEO & President Fernandez)
  • “Our increased business momentum is starting to reflect the relentless adoption of Agentic AI in everything we do.” (Chairman, CEO & President Fernandez)
  • “To help us lead that transformation, Dinesh Gupta joined MSCI last month as our new Chief Data Officer and Global Head of Operations.” (Chairman, CEO & President Fernandez)
  • “We closed one of the strongest first quarters in our history, reaffirming our traction across key initiatives, and we are growing our market share and expanding our influence in the increasingly AI-centric investment industry.” (Chief Financial Officer Andrew Wiechmann)

Outlook

  • “For Q2 2026, we currently expect Analytics year-over-year revenue growth to be roughly 5% for the quarter.” (CFO Wiechmann)
  • “For Q2, we expect to have an effective tax rate between 18% and 20%.” (CFO Wiechmann)
  • “The free cash flow outlook for the full year is unchanged, although Q2 is seasonally the highest quarter for cash tax payments for us.” (CFO Wiechmann)
  • Prior-quarter framing on long-term targets and disclosure shifted: “we remain committed to delivering the firm-wide long-term targets of low double-digit revenue growth, excluding ABF… However, we will no longer maintain product line-specific long-term targets.” (CFO Wiechmann, Q4 2025)

Financial Results

  • “The retention rate across all MSCI product lines was 95.4%.” (Chairman, CEO & President Fernandez)
  • “Index organic subscription run rate growth reaccelerated to low double-digit levels at over 10% with record Q1 recurring net new sales of $25 million, up 75% year-over-year.” (CFO Wiechmann)
  • “Asset-based fee run rate growth was 25%.” (CFO Wiechmann)
  • “Equity ETFs linked to our indexes captured a record $103 billion of inflows during the quarter, representing roughly 35% of all flows into equity index-linked ETFs.” (CFO Wiechmann)
  • “Our capital position remains strong with close to $400 million of cash on our balance sheet at the end of March.” (CFO Wiechmann)

Q&A

  • Alex Kramm, UBS Investment Bank, Research Division: “did anything slip given the environment… in Index and Analytics?” Henry Fernandez: “we have not seen clients pull back, we have not seen clients delay decisions, they've been operating on a business as usual.” (Chairman, CEO & President Fernandez)
  • Manav Patnaik, Barclays Bank PLC, Research Division: “Are you taking share?” Henry Fernandez: “we have… helped us… take market share away from competitors, especially in the sustainability and climate area.” (Chairman, CEO & President Fernandez)
  • Toni Kaplan, Morgan Stanley, Research Division: “any quantification around expense savings with regard to AI.” Henry Fernandez: “We are seeing significant early efficiencies in the use of AI across the whole board.” (Chairman, CEO & President Fernandez) Andy Wiechmann: “we are starting to see clients that are interested in licensing more content… for AI-driven use cases.” (CFO Wiechmann)
  • Owen Lau, Clear Street LLC: “outlook… for implementation?” Andy Wiechmann: “we did have a large implementation… and hence, you saw some meaningful nonrecurring revenues.” (CFO Wiechmann)
  • Ashish Sabadra, RBC Capital Markets, Research Division: “asset managers… moderated.” Andy Wiechmann: “we've taken more of an enterprise type approach… and that leads to some very attractive additional licensing opportunities.” (CFO Wiechmann)
  • Craig Huber, Huber Research Partners, LLC: “better momentum” in Sustainability and Climate. Henry Fernandez: “there is a lot of rationalization of cost… On climate, we're pretty hopeful… especially in physical risk.” (Chairman, CEO & President Fernandez)
  • Alexander EM Hess, JPMorgan Chase & Co, Research Division: “active ETF business.” Henry Fernandez: “we are very excited about that part of our business, very excited.” (Chairman, CEO & President Fernandez) Andy Wiechmann: “we can benefit both on the subscription side and the ABF side… It's very early days, so it's small for us.” (CFO Wiechmann)
  • Faiza Alwy, Deutsche Bank AG, Research Division: “custom indexes… sustainability.” Henry Fernandez: “the demand is very significant… we're leaving some money on the table.” (Chairman, CEO & President Fernandez)
  • Jinru Wu, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc., Research Division: “momentum behind the international inflows.” Andy Wiechmann: “we have seen a rotation take place… into international equities.” (CFO Wiechmann)
  • Scott Wurtzel, Wolfe Research, LLC: “what inning” in hedge funds. Andy Wiechmann: “we do believe it's attractive and sustainable.” (CFO Wiechmann)
  • David Motemaden, Evercore ISI Institutional Equities, Research Division: “economics” of IndexAI Insights. Andy Wiechmann: “The economics are generally consistent regardless of how clients access it.” (CFO Wiechmann)
  • Jason Haas, Wells Fargo Securities, LLC, Research Division: private credit risk impact. Henry Fernandez: “It's definitely a tailwind for us.” (Chairman, CEO & President Fernandez)
  • Kelsey Zhu, Autonomous Research US LP: AI competition. Henry Fernandez: “So far, we haven't seen any kind of competition or intense competition.” (Chairman, CEO & President Fernandez)

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment: slightly positive, with questions centered on sustainability of sales momentum, monetization of AI, and concentration of large deals, including “Are you taking share?” (Manav Patnaik, Barclays Bank PLC, Research Division) and “what inning we're at” (Scott Wurtzel, Wolfe Research, LLC).
  • Management sentiment: positive and confident in momentum and AI, including “we have not seen clients pull back” (Chairman, CEO & President Fernandez) and “we do believe it's attractive and sustainable” (CFO Wiechmann).
  • Versus prior quarter tone: Q4 2025 emphasized structural changes and targets, including “we will no longer maintain product line-specific long-term targets” (CFO Wiechmann, Q4 2025), while Q1 2026 placed more weight on execution, AI adoption, and recent bolt-on acquisitions.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Growth and operating metrics: Q1 2026 highlighted “a record asset-based fee run rate of $872 million” (Chairman, CEO & President Fernandez) versus Q4 2025 “total ABF run rate of $852 million” (Chairman & CEO Fernandez, Q4 2025).
  • Capital deployment: Q1 2026 buybacks were “more than $464 million” (Chairman, CEO & President Fernandez) versus Q4 2025 “nearly $958 million” (Chairman & CEO Fernandez, Q4 2025).
  • Strategic emphasis: Q1 2026 featured “three very exciting and highly strategic small bolt-on acquisitions” (Chairman, CEO & President Fernandez) and a new senior hire, “Chief Data Officer and Global Head of Operations” (Chairman, CEO & President Fernandez), while Q4 2025 centered on leadership transition: “Baer… will formally step down as President on March 1.” (Chairman & CEO Fernandez, Q4 2025)

Risks and Concerns

  • Geopolitical disruption: “Except for a slowdown… in the Gulf region… we have not seen any effect of the Iran war anywhere else in the world.” (Chairman, CEO & President Fernandez)
  • Segment headwinds: “In Real Assets, we still face some headwinds with our property transaction solutions.” (CFO Wiechmann)
  • Near-term Sustainability and Climate pressure: “We expect these pressures and the muted growth in Sustainability and Climate to continue in the near term.” (CFO Wiechmann)

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 2026 as a strong start driven by higher run rate growth, record equity ETF inflows, and reaccelerating Index subscription momentum, while positioning AI and recent bolt-on acquisitions as catalysts for faster product velocity and broader content licensing. The company maintained its full-year free cash flow outlook, guided Q2 Analytics revenue growth to roughly 5%, and said Sustainability and Climate demand remains pressured near term even as it cited competitive share gains and expanding physical-risk focus.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Synchrony projects $9.10-$9.50 2026 EPS while authorizing $6.5B buyback program
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Synchrony Financial (SYF) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “Synchrony started the year with strong momentum and delivered first quarter financial results that included record first quarter purchase volume of $43 billion.” (President, CEO & Director Brian Doubles)

  • “Synchrony’s co-branded credit cards, including our dual cards, accounted for 51% of our total purchase volume in the first quarter and increased 20% versus last year.” (President, CEO & Director Doubles)

  • “Synchrony continued to execute across our key strategic priorities during the first quarter, adding or renewing more than 15 partners, including Indian Motorcycle, Harbor Freight and Miracle Ear.” (President, CEO & Director Doubles)

  • “Synchrony generated $43 billion of purchase volume, a first quarter record and a 6% increase compared to last year.” (Executive VP & CFO Brian Wenzel)

  • “To summarize Synchrony’s first quarter results, we generated net earnings of $805 million or $2.27 per diluted share.” (Executive VP & CFO Wenzel)

  • “Our net charge-off rate was 5.42% in the first quarter, a decrease of 96 basis points from 6.38% in the prior year.” (Executive VP & CFO Wenzel)

  • “Our Board of Directors approved a new share repurchase program of up to $6.5 billion of the company’s common stock, which commences in the second quarter of 2026 and a change from our prior share repurchase program does not have an expiration date.” (Executive VP & CFO Wenzel)

Outlook

  • “We continue to expect accelerated growth in purchase volume and average active accounts without any further broad-based credit refinements as we move through the year.” (Executive VP & CFO Wenzel)

  • “The outcome should more than offset the impact of elevated payment rates to drive mid-single-digit growth in ending loan receivables by year-end.” (Executive VP & CFO Wenzel)

  • “This will be driven by growth in our core portfolio as well as a combination of both recently launched and soon-to-be launched programs, including Walmart OnePay, Bob’s Discount Furniture, RH and approximately $725 million of Lowe’s commercial co-brand loan receivables, which was added in early April.” (Executive VP & CFO Wenzel)

  • “We expect our net charge-offs to be less than 5.5% for the full year.” (Executive VP & CFO Wenzel)

  • “Putting all these elements together, Synchrony remains on track to deliver between $9.10 and $9.50 in diluted earnings per share.” (Executive VP & CFO Wenzel)

Financial Results

  • “Ending loan receivables were flat at $100 billion, though we did achieve a positive inflection in ending loan receivables with an increase of approximately $477 million at the end of the first quarter.” (Executive VP & CFO Wenzel)

  • “Net interest income increased 4% to $4.6 billion.” (Executive VP & CFO Wenzel)

  • “Our first quarter net interest margin increased 76 basis points versus last year to 15.5%.” (Executive VP & CFO Wenzel)

  • “Provision for credit losses decreased $156 million to $1.3 billion.” (Executive VP & CFO Wenzel)

  • “Other expense increased 6% to $1.3 billion, primarily driven by the costs related to technology investments and higher operational losses.” (Executive VP & CFO Wenzel)

Q&A

  • Terry Ma, Barclays Bank PLC, Research Division: asked what supports “mid-single digits” receivables growth confidence; Executive VP & CFO Wenzel: “we saw strong new account originations of 15% in the first quarter of this year” and “for the first couple of weeks in April, we’ve seen that to be consistent with how we exited to maybe slightly stronger from a purchase volume standpoint.”

  • Ryan Nash, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc., Research Division: asked what moves results within the $9.10-$9.50 EPS range; Executive VP & CFO Wenzel: “we’re not guiding inside the range,” and cited swing factors including payment-rate dynamics and delinquency performance, adding “we’ll have to watch the uncertainty as it relates to the geopolitical risk that exists today.”

  • Ryan Nash, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc., Research Division: asked about buyback pacing and Basel; Executive VP & CFO Wenzel: “we don’t give quarterly cadence,” and on Basel III said standardized would be favorable, “you would see our RWAs go down and our capital get relief of 125 to 150 basis points,” while enhanced risk-based approach “it’s a net negative for us if the rule is adopted exactly as is.”

  • Darrin Peller, Wolfe Research, LLC: pressed on expense growth and AI/agentic commerce; Executive VP & CFO Wenzel: operational losses were “a little bit more idiosyncratic in the first quarter and should reduce down as we move forward,” while President, CEO & Director Doubles said, on agentic commerce, “it’s a big area of focus for us,” and on GenAI adoption, “90% of the professional workforce is using it.”

  • L. Erika Penala, UBS Investment Bank, Research Division: asked if guidance embeds reserve releases; Executive VP & CFO Wenzel: “we haven’t given any view on whether or not we’ll be releasing reserves or not,” and added, “we continue to evaluate the environment and we step out quarter-by-quarter.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Slightly negative analyst tone, concentrated on capital return pace, Basel outcomes, expense growth discipline, and whether guidance implies hidden offsets (e.g., Erika Penala’s “it’s a fierce debate in the marketplace right now”).

  • Neutral-to-slightly positive management tone, emphasizing resilience and control while avoiding point commitments on cadence: “we don’t give quarterly cadence” and “we’re not guiding inside the range.” (Executive VP & CFO Wenzel)

  • Compared with the prior quarter, Q1 2026 Q&A stayed focused on capital/Basel and operating leverage, while the most pointed prior-quarter regulatory topic (“APR cap”) was not raised in this quarter’s questions.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Growth and engagement language strengthened as Q1 2026 opened with “record first quarter purchase volume of $43 billion” (President, CEO & Director Doubles), compared with Q4 2025 “more than $49 billion of purchase volume” and “a year-over-year increase of 3%.” (President, CEO & Director Doubles)

  • Capital-return framework changed as Q1 2026 introduced “a new share repurchase program of up to $6.5 billion… [that] does not have an expiration date,” (Executive VP & CFO Wenzel) whereas Q4 2025 emphasized returning capital “subject to… regulatory restrictions or expectations and our capital plan.” (Executive VP & CFO Wenzel)

  • Guidance level was reiterated rather than revised: Q1 2026 said “remains on track to deliver between $9.10 and $9.50,” (Executive VP & CFO Wenzel) consistent with Q4 2025: “expected to deliver net earnings per diluted share between $9.10 and $9.50.” (Executive VP & CFO Wenzel)

Risks and Concerns

  • “Other expense increased… primarily driven by the costs related to technology investments and higher operational losses,” alongside management’s expectation that operational losses were “idiosyncratic… and should reduce down as we move forward.” (Executive VP & CFO Wenzel)

  • On macro uncertainty, management tied range outcomes to “what happens in the macro environment” and cited “the uncertainty as it relates to the geopolitical risk that exists today.” (Executive VP & CFO Wenzel)

  • Management highlighted late-fee headwinds within NII expectations: “these trends will partially offset the lower late fee incidence.” (Executive VP & CFO Wenzel)

Final Takeaway

Synchrony’s management framed Q1 2026 as a momentum quarter—highlighting “record first quarter purchase volume of $43 billion,” a “positive inflection in ending loan receivables,” and full-year EPS guidance of “between $9.10 and $9.50,” while also announcing a new “up to $6.5 billion” buyback authorization without an expiration date. In Q&A, analysts repeatedly pressed on the durability of mid-single-digit receivables growth, expense discipline, Basel capital outcomes, and buyback pacing, while management consistently declined to provide a repurchase cadence and positioned 2026 performance around payment-rate dynamics, credit seasonality, and the ramp of programs including Walmart OnePay, Bob’s Discount Furniture, RH, and Lowe’s commercial co-brand receivables.
